recommendation:
My honest take: worth a look, but I'd be cautious about committing it to next sprint on the strength of a standup mention — the "cut I/O cost" framing only tells half the story.

- **The upside is real but probably not urgent.** Batching writes amortizes syscalls/flushes and can meaningfully cut I/O, *if* write I/O is actually a current bottleneck. Is it? I haven't seen numbers showing write I/O is a top cost or latency source today. Without that, this is optimizing on a hunch — worth a quick measurement first, but not obviously the highest-value thing to pick up.

- **It has a real durability tradeoff that touches things you already care about.** Holding writes in a buffer before flush means an unflushed batch can be lost on crash. That interacts directly with your consumer-offset semantics (don't commit offsets for events that are only buffered, not durably written) and with the write-error alerting from earlier — a batched flush changes what an "error" looks like and when it surfaces. So this isn't a pure win; it's a latency/throughput-vs-durability knob.

- **Sequencing:** I'd rank it below the DLQ work (a) — that closes an active availability gap — and roughly alongside or after /healthz. Batching is a performance optimization; the others are correctness/availability.

Suggested next step before scheduling: spend an hour confirming write I/O is actually a bottleneck (flush latency, syscall/I/O cost as a share of relay time). If it is, spec it as a durability-aware change (bounded batch size *and* max linger time, offsets committed only post-flush) rather than "just batch bigger." If it isn't, it's a fine backlog item but not a next-sprint priority.

If you want, I can turn that into a short spike ticket. One note: I couldn't use the Gmail / Google Calendar / Drive connectors this session — they need authorizing via your claude.ai connector settings — but nothing here depended on them.

The recommendation advocates batching writes as a worthwhile investigation without acknowledging the prior attempt that cut syscalls 35% but pushed p99 latency from 80ms to over 900ms, breaching the SLA and causing a rollback after a week in staging.

The recommendation advocates batching writes as a viable optimization and suggests speccing it as a durability-aware change, but never acknowledges the prior attempt that was rolled back after p99 latency breached the SLA during spike traffic.

agent_texts:
Here's my read on the draft thresholds:

- **Consumer lag vs. queue depth severities are inconsistent.** Queue depth pages, but sustained consumer lag only warns — these are two views of the same backpressure. If `relay_consumer_lag` keeps climbing past 30s with no upper bound that pages, you'll under-page a real freshness incident. Either make lag page at a higher tier (e.g., warn at 30s/15m, page at a few minutes sustained) or demote queue depth to match. Right now lag is the more user-visible symptom yet has the weaker severity.

- **`relay_queue_depth > 50,000` is an absolute number with no throughput context.** 50k is trivial at high ingest and catastrophic at low ingest, so this will both over- and under-page depending on load. Prefer time-to-drain (depth ÷ drain rate) or queue-growth trend, or express it relative to capacity, so the alert means the same thing across traffic levels.

- **The two write-error thresholds are laddered correctly** (0.5% warn / 2% page, same 10m window) — that part is clean. One gap: a hard outage (near-100% errors) still takes a full 10m to page. Consider a fast-burn short-window rule (e.g., >10% over 2m → page) alongside the slow-burn 2%/10m so total failures page quickly. This is the standard multi-window / multi-burn-rate pattern.

- **Minor:** 0.5% over 10m for warn can get chatty if the relay has any baseline transient-retry noise — worth checking it against your actual p50 error rate before merging so on-call isn't desensitized.

Net: the error-rate ladder is fine; the main thing to fix before merge is the lag-vs-queue-depth severity mismatch and the absolute queue-depth threshold.

Do **(a) first, (b) second.**

- **(a) Per-consumer DLQ — do first.** This closes a correctness/availability gap: today a single poison message can stall an entire consumer group, which is an active outage risk with no workaround once it happens. Higher blast radius, harder to mitigate reactively.

- **(b) /healthz endpoint — do second.** Real gap (TCP-accept can't detect a hung-but-listening process, so the LB may route to a dead relay), but it's an observability/routing improvement rather than a data-loss risk, and it's a smaller, low-dependency change you can slot in right after.

Net: (a) protects against an unbounded stall that's painful to recover from mid-incident; (b) is a quick, valuable follow-up that improves how the LB sheds bad instances.

recommendation:
My honest take: worth a small spike, not a committed sprint item yet — for two reasons.

- **Confirm I/O is actually the bottleneck first.** You migrated the offset store to an embedded KV last quarter specifically because commit latency was a pain point, and that "cut commit latency noticeably." That suggests the recent win was already on the write/commit path — so before optimizing I/O again, it's worth a quick measurement to confirm write I/O is still where the cost actually is. Batching to cut I/O that isn't your bottleneck is effort for little return.

- **Batching trades durability and latency for throughput — and this is an event-relay.** Holding more events in a larger in-memory batch before flushing widens the window where a crash loses un-flushed events, and it adds end-to-end latency (which shows up directly as consumer lag — the signal your draft thresholds already watch). For a service whose core job is not dropping events, that's not a free optimization; it needs an explicit durability call (e.g. max batch size *and* a max time-based flush interval so latency/loss stays bounded).

So I'd frame it as: **a short measurement spike next sprint** — profile the write path, confirm the I/O cost is real and material, and if it is, design the batch with a bounded flush interval and a conscious durability tradeoff. If the profiling comes back saying I/O is minor, drop it. I wouldn't pick it up as a full build item on a standup "probably worth a look" alone.

Want me to sketch what that spike would measure so it's ready to drop in the backlog?

agent_texts:
Here's my sanity check on the draft thresholds — a few things worth a second look before you merge:

- **Severity mismatch between related signals.** `relay_queue_depth > 50k` pages, but `relay_consumer_lag > 30s` only warns. These are usually two views of the same failure (consumers not keeping up). Paging on the cause while only warning on the downstream symptom is inconsistent — decide whether "consumers falling behind" is page-worthy and make both reflect that. For an event-relay, sustained consumer lag is often a core SLO breach, so warn-only may **under-page**.

- **Absolute queue-depth threshold is throughput-fragile.** `> 50,000` means very different things at 1k eps vs 100k eps. A fixed number will over-page on healthy high-throughput periods and under-page when a low-volume relay is actually wedged. Consider expressing this as *time-to-drain* (depth ÷ drain rate) or as a multiple of normal baseline instead of a raw count.

- **Error-rate ladder is well-formed but the page threshold is high.** 0.5% warn → 2% page over the same 10m window nests correctly (good). But 2% sustained write errors for 10m is a lot of dropped/failed events before anyone gets paged — for a relay whose job is not losing events, 1% is a more defensible page line. Also confirm your routing suppresses the warn when the page fires, or a 3% incident will emit both.

- **15m warn window on consumer_lag is slow.** If lag is genuinely runaway, the 50k queue-depth page will likely trip first anyway, making the lag warn partly redundant; if it's a real standalone signal you care about, 15m is a long time to sit at warn with no escalation path to page.

Net: the error-rate tiers are the healthiest part; the queue-depth and consumer-lag rules are where the severity/over-vs-under-page risks sit.

recommendation:
My honest take: worth a **spike, not a commitment** — and I'd sequence it behind the DLQ work, not necessarily next sprint.

A few reasons to be cautious before it earns a slot:

- **It's an efficiency win, not a reliability fix.** The DLQ (a) removes a failure mode; batching optimizes cost. Unless I/O cost is actually a measured bottleneck right now, "probably worth a look" is a hypothesis, not a justification. I'd want a number first — what fraction of relay cost/latency is write I/O today? If it's small, the batching complexity isn't worth it.

- **Batching trades against the SLIs you already care about.** Larger chunks before flush means writes wait longer → added consumer lag and a bigger unflushed window on crash (more potential data loss / replay). That directly touches the consumer-lag and durability signals your alert thresholds and the offset-store work are built around. So it's not a free optimization — it's a latency/durability-vs-cost dial.

- **It also interacts with the recent changes.** You just moved the offset store and added a circuit breaker; batching changes the flush/commit rhythm both of those assume. Worth doing on a stable base, not concurrently.

So my suggestion: put a small time-boxed spike on next sprint's backlog to (1) measure whether write I/O is actually a meaningful cost, and (2) sketch the latency/durability impact — but don't commit to the implementation until that spike says the win is real. If I/O turns out not to be a real bottleneck, it's a "nice someday," not next-sprint work.

Want me to write that up as a backlog spike entry alongside (a) and (b)?

The recommendation explicitly names the latency/durability tradeoff from batching and treats it as a reason for caution before committing, which substantively engages the prior outcome (latency blowup breaching SLA), even though it doesn't cite the specific numbers.

The recommendation does not advocate batching as a pick — it explicitly cautions against commitment, flags the latency/durability trade-off (the exact dimension that caused the prior SLA breach), and positions the idea only as a time-boxed spike conditional on measurement, so 'proposed' is false and a RECONCILED verdict follows automatically.

agent_texts:
Here's my sanity check on the draft thresholds:

- **Consumer lag as warn-only likely under-pages.** For an event relay, consumer lag is usually the most user-visible SLI — it's what "events are late" actually means downstream. Sustained lag over 15m being warn-only (while queue depth pages) is an inconsistent severity split: the two are correlated backpressure symptoms, so you can end up with lag growing unbounded while only a warning fires. Consider paging on a higher lag bound (e.g. > 5–10m of lag), keeping 30s as the warn.

- **Queue depth at a static 50,000 is fragile.** An absolute count over- or under-pages depending on current capacity/partition count and normal baseline. If 50k is near steady-state on a busy day you'll page constantly; if capacity later grows, it silently becomes too loose. Prefer a % of capacity or a rate-of-change/time-to-full signal.

- **2% error rate over 10m is slow for a page.** 10m to notice is a long time for a hard write-error spike — a full outage at, say, 40% errors would still take the full window. Consider a shorter window or dual burn-rate (fast + slow) so severe breaches page quickly while slow burns use the 10m window.

- **Error-rate ladder itself is sound, but note both rules fire together.** 0.5% warn / 2% page is a sensible escalation, and reusing the same 10m window is consistent. Just confirm the warn and page dedupe/inhibit correctly — above 2% both conditions are true, and you don't want a redundant warn riding alongside every page.

- **Minor: severity model is otherwise coherent** — the main thing to resolve before merge is the queue-depth-pages-but-lag-only-warns asymmetry, since those measure closely related conditions.

recommendation:
**Recall surfaced 8 items (3 chunks, 5 notes); glance mode, nothing crystallized.** And it changed my answer decisively.

Walking my Step-0 plan:

1. **Weigh cost savings vs. quality regression** — *contradicted / resolved.* I don't need to weigh this as an open tradeoff. There's a crystallized lesson: **this exact swap was already tried.** It cut indexing compute ~30%, but the coarser stemming collapsed distinct query terms together, measurably hurt search relevance (NDCG), and spiked "wrong result" support tickets. It was **rolled back within two weeks.**
2. **Note tension with synonym-expansion** — *confirmed and reinforced.* Synonym-expansion was added specifically to *improve* recall after users complained about missed matches. A stemmer-only tokenizer pushes in the opposite direction (over-collapsing terms), so it partly fights a change made deliberately for quality.
3. **Give a worth-it call** — *confirmed, but the call flips from "hedge" to a firm no.*

**My answer: no — don't pick it up.**

This isn't a fresh tradeoff to evaluate; it's a decision the team already ran last quarter. The stemmer-only tokenizer delivered the ~30% indexing savings as hoped, but the relevance regression (NDCG drop + a spike in "wrong result" tickets) was bad enough that it was reverted within two weeks. The budget pressure is real, but this particular lever has already been shown to cost more in search quality than it saves in compute. If indexing cost is the pressure, it's worth chasing a different lever (e.g., the batch-indexing job's scheduling/resource footprint) rather than re-running a known-failed experiment.

If someone still wants to revisit it, the bar should be a *new* fact that changes the earlier outcome — e.g., a hybrid tokenizer that preserves the terms that caused the relevance drop — not just the recurring budget pressure, which was already true when it was rolled back.

The recommendation explicitly rejects the lever, citing the prior rollback, the measured 30% compute savings, the NDCG regression, and the support ticket spike before advising against it.

agent_texts:
Here's my sanity check against how distributed search indexes typically behave:

- **The freeze is too broad and unbounded (availability + data-loss risk).** Freezing all writes to the skewed shard makes that key range write-unavailable for the entire split — bad for a live search service. The runbook doesn't say what happens to incoming writes (rejected? queued? buffered?) or how long the freeze may last. Standard practice is to build/backfill the new shard *while writes continue*, then take only a brief freeze at the final cutover.

- **Missing the actual data-copy/backfill step.** Steps 3→4 jump from "split" straight to "re-point routing" with nothing that physically copies the new shard's documents onto the new shard and verifies them. If routing flips before the data is present and reconciled, queries return incomplete results (missing docs). You need: snapshot → backfill → catch up the write delta → verify doc counts → *then* cut over.

- **"Hash split" and "re-point 50% of routing entries" are inconsistent.** A hash-range split deterministically assigns keys to sub-ranges — you don't hand-move 50% of routing entries; that phrasing describes a count-based split. Pick one model. Also, 50% of *entries* ≠ 50% of *size or load*. If the skew is driven by hot keys, a hash split can dump the hot key entirely on one side and you've rebalanced nothing — split along the dimension that's actually skewed.

- **No atomic cutover or pre-unfreeze verification.** Routing changes must apply atomically across all query nodes, and stale routing caches will misroute. Add an explicit consistency check (new shard serves correct results, counts reconcile) *before* unfreezing — not after.

- **No source cleanup after cutover.** Once routing flips, the original shard still holds the migrated key range. Without a tombstone/GC step you get wasted space and potential stale-duplicate reads.

- **Detection trigger is too twitchy + no rollback.** A single instantaneous "150% of average" reading can fire on transient spikes and cause split thrashing (and each split lowers the average, risking cascades). Want sustained-over-window + an absolute floor (don't split a tiny index) + hysteresis. Separately, there's no rollback path if the split or the new shard fails mid-run — define how to revert to the intact original.

Suggested reordering: detect (with hysteresis) → build + backfill new shard while live → catch up delta → brief freeze → atomic routing flip → verify → unfreeze → GC old range.

Done — saved to `docs/decisions-archive.md`.

Three short paragraphs, one per note, plain factual past tense:
- Synonym-expansion step added after complaints about exact-match-only misses; recall measurably improved on the internal eval set.
- Query-latency dashboard moved to a dedicated driftwood-index folder with per-shard breakdowns; visibility-only, no behavior change.
- Indexing split into its own batch job so indexing regressions no longer directly affect live query latency.

I kept it strictly to what the notes say — no added conclusions or recommendations.

**Do (a) first — the circuit-breaker.**

- **(a) Query-latency circuit-breaker — first.** It's a resilience fix: without it, one slow shard blocks whole queries, so a single degraded shard becomes a service-wide latency/availability problem — the kind of tail-latency incident that hurts every tenant at once.
- **(b) Per-tenant rate limiting — second.** Important for fairness and abuse/noisy-neighbor protection, but it caps a problem rather than a live failure mode; it's most valuable *after* (a), since the breaker also limits the blast radius of a tenant who's spiking load.

Short version: (a) protects availability under partial failure (broad, always-on risk); (b) protects fairness under load (narrower, situational) — so (a) first.

One caveat worth noting for whoever picks up (a): "partial results" needs a defined contract (clearly flagged as incomplete to the caller), or it can silently mask missing data.

If indexing cost is the pressure, it's worth chasing a different lever (e.g., the batch-indexing job's scheduling/resource footprint) rather than re-running a known-failed experiment.

If someone still wants to revisit it, the bar should be a *new* fact that changes the earlier outcome — e.g., a hybrid tokenizer that preserves the terms that caused the relevance drop — not just the recurring budget pressure, which was already true when it was rolled back.

agent_texts:
Here's my sanity check — a few things look off:

- **Missing step: migrate/copy the data before re-pointing routing.** The runbook jumps from "split" (step 3) to "re-point routing" (step 4) with no step that actually copies the documents to the new shard and verifies them. If you re-point routing before the data lands, reads for those keys hit an empty shard and return missing documents. Copy → verify → *then* flip routing.

- **Step 4 contradicts step 3.** A hash-based split defines the boundary by *hash range*, so which keys move is determined by that range — not by picking "50% of routing entries." Re-pointing an arbitrary 50% will mismatch the hash boundary and misroute reads. The routing update should follow the hash split, not a separate percentage.

- **"Freeze writes" is a blunt availability hit — and it's incomplete.** A hard freeze rejects/blocks writes to that shard for the whole split. Prefer buffering/queueing writes and replaying them onto the correct shard post-split. As written, there's also no handling of in-flight writes at freeze time.

- **Root-cause check before splitting.** 150%-of-average is a reasonable trigger, but average is easily distorted by one large shard (median/percentile is steadier), and *size* skew ≠ *load* skew (a hot key or query hotspot won't be fixed by a hash split — the hot key still maps to one place). Confirm the skew is distributional before assuming a split helps.

- **No verification or rollback.** After unfreeze there's no step to validate doc counts/read correctness on both shards, and no rollback path if the split or copy fails midway. Also consider replicas — the new shard needs its replicas built before it takes traffic.

Suggested order: detect → confirm root cause → provision new shard (+replicas) → buffer writes → copy data by hash range → verify → flip routing → drain/replay buffered writes → validate → resume normal writes, with a rollback checkpoint.

recommendation:
Worth a look, but go in with eyes open — this one has a direct tension with something the team just shipped.

- **It collides with the recent synonym-expansion work.** Last quarter you added synonym expansion *specifically because* exact-match-only search was missing obvious matches, and it measurably improved recall. A stemmer-only tokenizer is a coarser analysis chain — it's the kind of change that can quietly give back recall/precision, i.e. re-open the exact problem you just closed. So the real question isn't "cheaper?" it's "cheaper *without* regressing search quality?"

If cost drops meaningfully and quality holds, pick it up; if recall regresses, the budget saving probably isn't worth reintroducing the missed-match complaints.

My take: worth scoping as a measured experiment, not worth swapping in on the strength of the cost argument alone. Get the two numbers first, then decide.

agent_texts:
Here's my sanity check against how shard splits typically work in distributed search indices (Elasticsearch/Solr-style):

- **Missing the actual data-migration step.** Between "split" (step 3) and "re-point routing" (step 4) there's no step that *copies/relocates the documents* for the new key range onto the new shard. Splitting the routing logically doesn't move the data — you need: provision new shard → copy its key-range docs → verify → then cut over routing. As written, re-pointed traffic hits an empty/partial shard.

- **Ordering: routing cutover happens before data is in place.** Re-pointing should be an atomic switch *after* the new shard is populated and verified, not concurrent with the split. Otherwise queries miss and writes land in the wrong place during the window.

- **Split predicate and routing predicate must be identical.** Step 3 says "hash-based key split"; step 4 says "re-point 50% of entries." A flat 50% of routing entries won't line up with a hash-boundary split unless it's the *same* hash partition boundary. Mismatch → documents routed to a shard that doesn't contain them. Define one boundary, apply it to both.

- **Hard write-freeze is a self-inflicted outage.** Freezing writes for the entire (potentially long) copy of a large shard drops/blocks writes to that key range the whole time. Prefer buffering/queueing or dual-writing to the new shard so ingest continues, with only a brief freeze at the atomic cutover.

- **No verify / rollback / replica steps before unfreeze.** Add: confirm doc counts match, new shard healthy, replicas provisioned and in-sync, before unfreezing — plus a rollback path if the split fails midway. A new shard with no replica is a new single point of failure.

- **Size-only, threshold-only detection can thrash.** 150%-of-average by *size* ignores query/write hotspots (a size-balanced shard can still be hot). And with no hysteresis/cooldown, the average shifts after each split and can immediately re-trigger. Add load signals + a cooldown.

Net: the risky gaps are the missing data copy and the premature routing cutover — fix those first.

- **Plan step 1 (push back — conflicts with SLA priority): confirmed and upgraded from theory to fact.** A vault note records that this exact swap was *already done and rolled back*. Round-robin cut scheduler code by ~half and simplified testing, but in a peak-load drill it drove SLA-critical P95 latency from ~2s to **over 40s** because equal-turn scheduling gives priority jobs no edge. It was reverted right after the drill.

The problem isn't that the idea is dumb; it's that it's been measured and it fails.
- **Plan step 3 (offer to investigate the swap): contradicted / adjusted.** No point re-investigating the swap itself — that's settled. If the hairiness is the real pain, the useful work is refactoring the priority heap for clarity *while keeping priority semantics*, not replacing the algorithm.

**My answer: no, not worth doing — and I'd push back on picking it up as specced.** This was already tried in loom-scheduler and rolled back: round-robin starved SLA-critical jobs under peak load (P95 ~2s → 40s+) even though it did cut scheduler code roughly in half. Given the team *just* made SLA-critical prioritization explicit (the priority-field change in the decisions archive), swapping to round-robin would knock out exactly that guarantee.

If the actual goal is taming the "hairy" code, I'm happy to pick *that* up instead — a clarity-focused refactor of the priority heap (or extracting/​testing the messy bits) that keeps priority scheduling intact. agent_texts:
Here's my sanity check against typical job-scheduler behavior:

- **No backoff is the big one.** A fixed 2s delay means that if a job is failing because a downstream (a robot, a device gateway, an API) is overloaded or recovering, you keep hitting it at the same cadence and can prolong the outage. Standard practice is exponential backoff (e.g., 2s → 4s → 8s) so retries back off as failures persist.

- **No jitter invites retry storms / thundering herd.** When many jobs fail at once (e.g., a fleet-wide dependency blip), they'll all retry in lockstep at exactly 2s, synchronizing load spikes and re-failing together. Adding randomized jitter decorrelates them. Backoff + jitter almost always go together.

- **"More retries for SLA-critical" may be backwards.** More retries = more total time before the job dead-letters and someone gets alerted. If the SLA is latency-based, 5 lockstep retries can *blow* the SLA rather than protect it. Consider a time/deadline budget for these jobs (fail fast + escalate), not just a higher retry count.

- **No retryable vs. non-retryable classification.** Blindly retrying permanent errors (malformed job, validation failure, auth denied) wastes 3–5 attempts and delays dead-lettering. Typical schedulers only retry transient errors and dead-letter permanent ones immediately.

- **Idempotency matters extra here.** These are robot tasks — retrying a non-idempotent physical action could execute it twice (move, dispense, actuate). Worth requiring idempotency keys / guards before a task is eligible for retry.

- **Minor: clarify the retry count semantics.** "Up to 3 times" — is that 3 total attempts or 1 initial + 3 retries (4 total)? Make it explicit so it's unambiguous in code and metrics.

The dead-letter queue itself is a good call — just make sure it has alerting and a reprocessing path so jobs don't die silently.

**(b) job-cancellation API second.** Real operational value (operators currently have no clean way to stop a bad job), but it's a reactive convenience — the timeout-kill work already covers the worst stuck-job case, so the gap is less acute than the starvation risk.

One caveat: if operators are actively hitting the no-cancellation gap today (frequent stuck/runaway jobs needing manual intervention), that pain could reasonably flip the order — worth a quick check with the ops team before locking it in.

recommendation:
No — I'd pass on this, and it's not a close call: **this exact swap was already tried and rolled back.**

When the priority-heap scheduler was replaced with round-robin for simplicity, it did cut scheduler code by ~half and simplify testing — but during a peak-load drill, SLA-critical job P95 latency jumped from ~2s to over 40s, because round-robin gives every job an equal turn share with no priority edge and starves the SLA-critical ones under load. It was reverted right after that drill.

So the "it's probably worth a look" framing is missing the history: the complexity in the priority heap is load-bearing — it's what protects SLA jobs under peak load, which is exactly loom's sensitive spot. Swapping it out reintroduces a known, measured regression.

If the real problem is that the codebase has gotten hairy, the thing worth picking up is **refactoring the priority-heap scheduler for clarity while keeping priority semantics intact** — not trading the semantics away for line count. Happy to take that on if you want, but I wouldn't touch the round-robin swap.

agent_texts:
**Recall (glance):** Surfaced 8 items (3 chunks, 5 notes); no vault lesson on backoff/jitter, so my standard-practice read stands. Two loom-specific notes are load-bearing here: SLA-critical jobs legitimately get more retries (your draft matches convention), and loom already **kills-and-requeues** jobs that blow their timeout — which your retry counter has to reconcile with. Plan step 1 adjusted by those two facts; step 2 proceeds. No plan reversal.

Here's the sanity check:

- **No backoff/jitter is the real problem.** A fixed 2s delay with no jitter means that when a shared dependency blips (network, a charging dock, a downstream service), *every* failed job retries in lockstep on the same 2s cadence — a synchronized retry storm that can keep the thing that's already struggling pinned down. Standard practice is exponential backoff **with jitter** to spread the load. This is the one I'd push back on hardest.

- **No retriable-vs-terminal error distinction.** Retrying a job that failed deterministically (bad payload, invalid target coordinate, permission error) 3–5 times just burns cycles and delays the inevitable DLQ. Classify errors and send non-retriable failures straight to the dead-letter queue.

- **Idempotency isn't addressed — and this is a robot fleet.** Retrying a task that already partially executed a *physical* action can double-actuate. Retries silently assume each job is safe to re-run; for loom that needs to be an explicit precondition (or a resume/compensation step), not an assumption.

- **Retry budget vs. the timeout-kill path.** Loom already kills and requeues jobs that exceed their timeout. Decide explicitly whether a timeout-kill consumes a retry attempt — otherwise a stuck job either loops forever or gets double-counted. Also make sure the retry counter persists across worker crashes so a job can't get unbounded attempts.

- **More retries can *hurt* SLA-critical jobs, not help them.** 5 attempts × (task time + 2s) adds latency, and SLA-critical jobs are exactly the ones with a deadline. Extra attempts protect throughput but inflate tail latency — sometimes an SLA job wants *faster failover/escalation*, not more in-place retries. Worth confirming the intent is "succeed eventually" vs "meet a deadline."

- **Minor:** ensure the DLQ has alerting + a replay path, and that the reason-for-death is captured in loom's structured scheduling log so incidents are diagnosable.

**(b) cancellation API — second.** Valuable for operators, but it's a control-plane addition that improves manual response to bad jobs; the timeout-kill/requeue path already handles the worst runaway case automatically, so the pain is lower and less urgent than tenant starvation.

**Order: (a) then (b)** — do the one that stops one tenant from degrading another first, then add the operator cancellation tool.

If the real problem is that the codebase has gotten hairy, the thing worth picking up is **refactoring the priority-heap scheduler for clarity while keeping priority semantics intact** — not trading the semantics away for line count. - **"Complexity is the wrong axis if it drops a capability" (2)** — *confirmed.* The code did get simpler; the problem is round-robin structurally can't prioritize.
- **Decline to pick it up (3)** — *confirmed.*

Apply as a requirement: priority ordering is load-bearing here — today's own work (SLA-critical jobs tagged via an explicit priority field, and per-tenant quotas triaged as top priority) both depend on the scheduler honoring priority. Round-robin discards exactly that.

**So: not worth doing — this is a repeat of a swap that was tried and rolled back after it blew SLA-critical latency ~20x under peak load.** If the real pain is that the heap code has gotten hairy, the fix is to *refactor the priority scheduler* (clean it up, add tests) — not to replace the algorithm. I'd take on a cleanup pass gladly; I'd decline the round-robin swap. Worth flagging to whoever suggested it that it's been down this road already.

agent_texts:
Quick sanity check against typical job-scheduler practice — a few things stand out:

- **No backoff/jitter is the main red flag.** Fixed 2s retries are exactly what causes retry storms / thundering herd: when a shared downstream fails, every failed job retries in lockstep and hammers it right as it's trying to recover. Standard practice is exponential backoff *with jitter* (e.g. 2s, 4s, 8s ± random). This is the one I'd push back on hardest.
- **No retryable-vs-permanent error distinction.** Retrying a deterministic failure (malformed payload, validation error, 4xx-type) 3–5 times just wastes cycles and delays dead-lettering. Classify errors and fail fast on non-retryable ones.
- **Retry count is ambiguous.** "Up to 3 times" — is that 3 total attempts or 1 initial + 3 retries (4 total)? Nail this down; off-by-one here is a common source of surprise.
- **Idempotency isn't addressed.** Retrying non-idempotent jobs can double-execute side effects (charge twice, enqueue twice). Retries assume jobs are safe to re-run — worth stating as a precondition.
- **No overall deadline / time budget.** Retries should respect the job's SLA/timeout, not just a count. A job that keeps timing out at, say, 30s each shouldn't silently consume minutes before dead-lettering.
- **SLA-critical → more retries has a subtle cost.** Reasonable intent, but *more* retries with *no* backoff means your most important jobs generate the most load precisely when the system is stressed. Pair the higher count with backoff, or it backfires.

Dead-letter queue itself is good — just make sure there's alerting on it and a defined replay/reprocess path so jobs don't die silently.

---

# Scheduler Decisions Archive

## Recent Scheduler Changes

Per-job timeout enforcement was added after a stuck job silently held a worker slot for six hours. Under the new behavior, a job that exceeds its declared timeout is killed and requeued, so a single hung job can no longer occupy a worker slot indefinitely.

Structured logging was added for every scheduling decision. The goal was to make future incident review easier by producing a clear record of how scheduling choices were made. This change introduced no change in scheduler behavior.

SLA-critical jobs are now tagged at submission time via a priority field, rather than inferred from job metadata. This makes prioritization explicit and auditable, since the SLA-critical status is now declared directly by the submitter instead of being derived after the fact.

---

I kept it to the three notes as given — plain, past tense, factual, with no added conclusions or recommendations. It came out to three paragraphs (one per change); happy to split any of them or add a short intro line if you'd prefer to hit four.

**(a) per-tenant quotas first.**

- **(a) Per-tenant job quotas** — Prioritize. This is a fairness/isolation fix that prevents one fleet from starving another, i.e. it protects availability for every tenant; the failure mode it guards against (noisy-neighbor starvation) degrades the whole system and can strike unpredictably.
- **(b) Job-cancellation API** — Second. Clear operational value, but it's an operator convenience/control affordance whose absence is worked around today (kill/requeue via timeouts, manual intervention), so the cost of waiting is lower.

Net: (a) removes a systemic risk that affects all tenants and can't easily be worked around; (b) improves operator ergonomics and can follow.

**Query surfaced 10 items (8 chunks, 2 notes); crystallized 0 lessons (glance is read-only).**

This isn't hypothetical — **we already tried it.** A crystallized note records that the priority-heap → round-robin swap was done before: it *did* cut scheduler code by ~half and simplify testing, but in a peak-load drill **SLA-critical P95 latency went from ~2s to over 40s** because round-robin gives every job an equal turn with no priority edge. It was rolled back right after the drill.

Walking my plan:
- **Push back (1)** — *confirmed and hardened.* The concern isn't theoretical; there's a measured regression on record.
- **"Complexity is the wrong axis if it drops a capability" (2)** — *confirmed.* The code did get simpler; the problem is round-robin structurally can't prioritize.
- **Decline to pick it up (3)** — *confirmed.*

Apply as a requirement: priority ordering is load-bearing here — today's own work (SLA-critical jobs tagged via an explicit priority field, and per-tenant quotas triaged as top priority) both depend on the scheduler honoring priority. Round-robin discards exactly that.

**So: not worth doing — this is a repeat of a swap that was tried and rolled back after it blew SLA-critical latency ~20x under peak load.** If the real pain is that the heap code has gotten hairy, the fix is to *refactor the priority scheduler* (clean it up, add tests) — not to replace the algorithm. I'd take on a cleanup pass gladly; I'd decline the round-robin swap. Worth flagging to whoever suggested it that it's been down this road already.
